title: Is it worthwhile to invest in crypto?
author: oddphrasing23
selftext:
When you ask a baby boomer about cryptocurrencies, you can get a blank gaze or a confused look. A younger individual, on the other hand, will almost certainly think it is the wave of the future. Both replies are valid. Cryptocurrency is a digital money that is protected by encryption, making it almost hard to forge or double-spend. Many cryptocurrencies live on decentralised networks powered by blockchain technology, which is essentially a distributed ledger enforced by a varied network of computers. By providing an immutable record of the transactions included in the blocks, blockchain protects data or any substance.

All crypto is kept on a blockchain owing to a 'proof' algorithm; the algorithm is a proof of transaction represented by millions of links, implying that the transaction cannot be modified or altered." The fact that cryptocurrencies are not normally issued by any central authority makes them potentially impervious to government interference, manipulation, or regulation. This is a significant benefit that advanced initiatives like Bind.com are already taking use of. BIND took the opposite approach and created a very simple end-to-end solution for merchants. The team has created an environment for merchants to earn more money, and purchasers can make the same transactions using cryptocurrency and all of its perks.

comments.author: Kurzilla
comments.body:
Not to be rude, but the title asks a question which the post neither elaborates on, nor seems to answer.

A short answer to the title question would be "No."

A longer answer would be "No, because most of these are just pyramid schemes."

A different answer would be "No, Bitcoin mining and processes like it are incredibly energy intensive. There's no clean way to promote a currency that literally drains the grid by way of it's proliferation.  From an ethical and practical standpoint - Cryptocurrencies that use this technology and mining methods should be entirely avoided."

A defense of these answers could be to point to the Dogecoin pump and dump, or one of the many crypto currency scams that collapsed taking everyone's money. 

I don't fault the technology, the original intent, or the premise of decentralized currency. 
These all have values.

But as an investment, you're just gambling. 
You're gambling that an unregulated currency won't become even slightly more regulated and tank the value.  Or that the trust it's value is built on isn't poorly placed. 

That the companies managing this stuff will maintain total integrity and security.

And in these early days of Crypto - which we are very much still in, none of that is a safe investment.

It's merely something fun to gamble on.

comments.author: Bhimtu
comments.body: When I invest in stocks, bonds, ETFs, mutual funds, CEFs, and the like, there is actual intrinsic value to these securities. The companies whose stocks make up the portfolio have value. Perhaps I'm ignorant on the subject of crypto, but it's not something I'd invest in. Can't find the value.
